unitTitle: KS2 Y6 Communication and collaboration (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Internet addresses
lessons.objectives:
I can recognise that computers use agreed rules (protocols) to send data
I can explain that devices on the internet have addresses (IP addresses)
I can explain how a DNS turns a website name into an IP address
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Words on the board: protocol · address · IP address · DNS. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the activity worksheet ready. Read the three 'I can…' aloud, slowly.
1. (0–8) Starter — how we use the internet. Show the word cloud. Every time we use the internet, data is sent between computers. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what sends data (a paper book is the odd one out).
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — a protocol is an agreed rule. We walk on one side of the corridor; we line up. An agreed rule = a protocol; computers use them too. Likely error: 'a protocol is a computer' → fix-words 'a protocol is an agreed RULE.' Support choose what a protocol is.
3. (18–32) Addresses. Show the postbox. A letter needs an address (name, street, town, postcode). Every device on the internet has an address — an IP address (e.g. 93.184.216.34). Pupils choose what an IP address is and why a message needs one.
4. (32–48) You-do — DNS. Show the address book. Numbers are hard to remember; names (bbc.co.uk) are easy. A DNS is like an address book: name → number. Pupils ORDER the steps of how a website name becomes an IP address; with the teacher, look up one real website's IP and screenshot it.
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what a DNS does (name → number).
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Data packets
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that data is sent over networks in small parts called packets
I can name the two main parts of a packet: the header and the data payload
I can explain that all data is sent in packets
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: packet · header · data payload. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: every device has an IP address.
1. (0–8) Starter — big data, small parts. Show the globe. A computer breaks data into small parts called packets; small parts are quicker to send. Pupils choose what a packet is; Core: why break data up?
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — the two parts of a packet. HEADER = the to/from addresses, the packet number, the total. DATA PAYLOAD = the actual data. Like a letter in an envelope (envelope = header, letter = payload). Likely error: 'the header is the message' → fix-words 'the header is the ENVELOPE; the payload is the LETTER.' Pupils SORT the parts into header / payload.
3. (22–40) You-do — packets are numbered. Pupils ORDER three numbered packets to read 'data is transferred in packets', then explain why each packet needs a number. Challenge: how does a computer know a packet is missing?
4. (40–50) All data travels in packets. Not just text — pictures, video and sound too. Pupils choose the best statement (all data is sent in packets). Screenshot their ordered packets.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil names the two parts of a packet.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: The internet — a global network (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Working together
lessons.objectives:
I can open and work on a file that is shared online
I can explain that the internet lets people share different media
I can work with others online to make something together
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: collaborate · share · online · Chat · Explore. TA: internet devices ready; each group's shared slide deck set up; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: packets carry all kinds of data.
1. (0–10) Starter — working far apart. People who are not together can still work together. Pupils SORT ways (shared online file, email, video call, letter, travel) into uses-the-internet / does not. Draw out: a shared file lets two people work on the SAME thing at once.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — a shared file online. Show the Explore panel. The whole group opens the same slide deck. Explore = find pictures and facts; Chat = message the group. Demonstrate opening the deck, Explore, and Chat.
3. (20–45) You-do — a country fact file. Pupils work with their group but stay at their own computer (as if in different places). They add text and a picture and use Chat. Pupils ORDER the 'add a picture with Explore' steps; screenshot their slide. Likely error: editing the wrong group's deck → fix-words 'open YOUR group's deck only.'
4. (45–52) Review — what media did you share? Text, images, maybe video/sound. Could you share all that by phone? (No — you can talk but not share files.)
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil names one collaboration tool (Chat / Explore).
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt with the Explore button; do not make the slide for them.

resources.title: Explore panel in a shared slide deck (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Shared working
lessons.objectives:
I can identify different ways of working together online
I can recognise that working online can be public or private
I can reuse and remix work, and know when it is OK
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: reuse · remix · public · private · copyright. TA: paper and pencils ready; Scratch logins ready; the Mars Lander project link ready; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we made something together online.
1. (0–10) Starter — build on a drawing. Pupils draw a simple picture (name on it), pass it to a partner who adds to it. You have reused and changed someone's work. Support choose what 'remix' means.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — remixing in Scratch. Show the Mars Lander with See inside and Remix. See inside = look at the blocks; Remix = make your own copy. One starter project → a different version for everyone. Likely error: 'remix changes the original' → fix-words 'remix makes your OWN copy.'
3. (22–42) You-do — remix the Mars Lander. Pupils log in, open the project, click See inside then Remix, and change the sprite/colours/sounds/blocks. Pupils ORDER the remix steps; tick what they changed; screenshot their remix.
4. (42–50) Is it always OK? Working online can be public or private. You need permission and should say whose work it was — copyright. 'It's always OK to use someone's work' — true or false? (False.)
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what Remix does (makes my own copy).
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

resources.title: Mars Lander — See inside and Remix (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: How we communicate
lessons.objectives:
I can name different ways people communicate
I can name ways to communicate over the internet
I can choose a way to communicate that suits a job
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: communicate · message · internet · purpose. TA: the starter worksheet open; whiteboards for the class list. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: the internet connects people far apart.
1. (0–8) Starter — so many ways to say 'Yes'. Nod, thumbs up, say it, write it. Pupils write two non-speaking ways. Then: which ways fail when you are far apart?
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — when we are far apart. Show the globe. A nod or thumbs up needs to be seen; the internet works over any distance. Support choose which way works far away.
3. (18–30) Communicating over the internet. Build a class list: a message, a video call, an email, a shared post. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the internet ways (shouting across the playground is the odd one out).
4. (30–45) You-do — choose the best way. Quick class notice → message to everyone; family abroad face to face → video call; send a report → email. Pupils MATCH each job to the best way, then make a group mind map for their scenario (keep for next lesson) and screenshot it.
5. (45–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil names one internet way.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: The internet connects people (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Communicating responsibly
lessons.objectives:
I can compare different ways of communicating on the internet
I can decide when I should and should not share information online
I can explain that communication online may not be private
I can say what to do if I see something upsetting online
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: public · private · one-to-one · one-to-many · report. TA: the starter worksheet open; pupils' mind maps from Lesson 5. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. End-of-unit lesson — recap the whole journey at the end.
1. (0–10) Starter — how do you communicate? Quick stand-up recap (video called / messaged / shared a picture — a movement starter). Show the communicating image. Introduce public vs private; Support choose what a private message is.
2. (10–28) I-do → we-do — comparing ways. Public or private (who can see it)? One-to-one or one-to-many (one person or many)? Pupils SORT ways into public/private and one-to-one/one-to-many on the activity worksheet, using familiar examples (a text vs a public video).
3. (28–40) You-do — think before you share. Online messages are not always private. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what to keep private (address, password, full name and school — not favourite colour). Core: why think before you post?
4. (40–50) If you see something upsetting. Pupils ORDER the safety steps: stop / don't share → turn off or minimise → tell a trusted adult → report it together. Link to the school's online-safety rules (e.g. SMART). Keep it calm and matter-of-fact.
5. (50–55) End of unit — recap: addresses → packets → working together → remixing → ways to communicate → staying safe. Self-tick the four 'I can…'; exit ticket: three top tips before communicating online.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
